summary: Cathy O'Neil examines the 2008 D.C. cheating scandal under Michelle Rhee's tenure as chancellor, where high-stakes testing with cash bonuses led to widespread cheating evidenced by suspiciously high erasure rates on standardized tests. While Atlanta teachers were jailed for similar cheating under comparable incentive structures, the D.C. investigation was effectively buried. O'Neil argues the fundamental flaw wasn't the teachers who cheated but the high-stakes regime itself that predictably created perverse incentives. She criticizes Rhee for readily firing teachers while refusing to acknowledge that her celebrated reform successes were built on fraudulent test results.
key_points: Cash bonuses of $8,000-$10,000 for teachers and principals were tied to student test score improvements in high-poverty D.C. schools
Erasure rates on tests averaged 12.7 per test versus the normal rate of less than 1, indicating systematic answer changes
The investigation into cheating was 'smothered' unlike Atlanta where teachers were imprisoned for similar offenses
The high-stakes testing regime itself created the conditions that made cheating predictable and rational
Rhee fired hundreds of teachers, sometimes without explanation, while her own 'successes' were based on fraudulent data
money_quotes: Certain high poverty schools weren't doing so well. Michelle Rhee came in as chancellor and suggested that the teachers and principals simply needed some more incentives to achieve better student learning.
An investigation followed but came up pretty empty.
I'm not suggesting we want more jailings, by the way. I'm suggesting that the original high-stakes regime was fundamentally flawed and naturally gave rise to the cheating in the first place.
Michelle Rhee's legacy was one were she was very happy to fire people but very reluctant to admit that her educational reform successes were based on lies.
key_insight: High-stakes testing regimes that attach large financial rewards and punishments to metrics predictably corrupt those metrics, and the architects of such systems rarely face accountability for the fraud they incentivize.
